---
title: Bayesian Graphical Lasso for Estimating Brain Networks
author: Liam Hanson, BIOS 731
output: 
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "dolphin"
    fonttheme: "structurebold"
bibliography: references.bib
---
# Overview 

- Discuss why we might want to estimate a graph

- Discuss the Bayesian Graphical Lasso Gibbs Sampler

- Review results from a small simulation study

# Motivation

We are interested in viewing the brain as a graph i.e. a set of nodes, say regions of the brain, and edges, the connections between these nodes

```{r, out.width="60%", fig.cap= "[@Hendricksen2015VisualizingDB]"}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "13-Figure1.6-1.png")
)
```

Different brains have different network structures and therefore different local and global properties of these networks

# Motivation

In general, healthy brains have a so-called “small-world” or “scale-free” structure.

Each node has few neighbors but it is easy for one group of nodes or sub-network to talk to another (six degrees of separation). 

This implies the existence of hub nodes,
nodes through which other areas of the network communicate.

# Motivation 

```{r, out.width="60%", out.height="60%", fig.cap= "[@Bullmore2009]"}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "41583_2009_Article_BFnrn2575_Fig3_HTML.png")
)
```
A contrasting example is in people with Schizophrenia. These individuals
tend to score lower on these small-world metrics. 
 
# Graph Estimation

We don't observe these graphs directly

```{r, out.width="50%", out.height = "50%", fig.cap="[@Buckner2013]"}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "41593_2013_Article_BFnn3423_Fig1_HTML.png")
)
```

Instead, we have time series data for each voxel or region of interest in the
brain where the measured quantity is the so-called BOLD signal.

We will use the associations between these time series to estimate our graph.

# Graph Estimation

A graph can be represented as an adjacency matrix: a symmetric matrix $A$ with
$a_{ij} = 1$ if an edge is present between node $i,j$ and $a_{ij} = 0$ otherwise.

For our purposes, we assume that at time step (i.e. scan) $t$, the value of the 
BOLD signal across all $D$ nodes $Y_D \sim MVN(0, \Sigma)$.

Fact: The precision matrix $\Omega = \Sigma^{-1}$ has the property that its entries
$\omega_{ij}$ encode the partial correlation between node $i$ and node $j$. 

If $\omega_{ij} = 0$, these nodes are conditionally independent. Generally,
we expect the true precision matrices to be sparse (recall scale free 
organization).

# Graphical Lasso

- To capture this sparsity, something like LASSO (Tibshirani 1996)
could work.

- We could fit successive pairwise LASSO's and regress the residuals to obtain 
partial correlations, but this isn't ideal

- Instead, let's maximize the penalized Multivariate Gaussian log-likelihood
with respect to the $\Omega$:

$$\textrm{log (det }\Omega) - \textrm{tr}(\frac{S}{n}\Omega) - \rho||\Omega||_1$$
where $S$ is the sample covariance matrix and $||\Omega||_1$ is the entry-wise
$l_1$-norm of $\Omega$. 

# Graphical Lasso

- This is the approach of @friedman2007sparseinversecovarianceestimation


- At a high level, it recursively fits a modified LASSO in a blockwise fashion
using sub-gradient methods and coordinate descent (gets around non-differentiability of $l_1$ norm.)


- The focus of my project was a Bayesian Gibbs-Sampler approach to the Graphical Lasso 
by @10.1214/12-BA729 to estimate the aforementioned graphs

# Bayesian Graphical Lasso via Gibbs Sampling

We have the likelihood and prior
$$p(\textbf{y}_t | \Omega) = MVN(\textbf{y}_t|0, \Omega^{-1} )$$
$$p(\Omega|\lambda) = C^{-1} \prod_{i<j}\textrm{DE}(\omega_{ij}|\lambda)\prod_{i=1}^p
\textrm{Exp}(\omega_{ii}|\frac{\lambda}{2})\cdot1_{\Omega\in M^+}$$

where $M^+$ is the space of positive-definite matrices. 

- DE is the double-exponential (Laplace) distribution used in classic Bayesian LASSO
which induces potential shrinkage to 0.
- Natural exponential prior on the diagonal elements i.e. inverted variances.

# Bayesian Graphical Lasso

- Fact: The double exponential results from marginalizing over a mixture of 
normals with variances $\tau_{ij}$ and exponential mixing density. 

- This results in the posterior distribution:

$$p(\Omega, \tau|\textbf{Y}, \lambda) \propto |\Omega|^{\frac{n}{2}}\textrm{ exp\{-tr }
(\frac{1}{2}S\Omega)\}\prod_{i<j}\{\tau_{ij}^{-\frac{1}{2}}\textrm{exp}(-\frac{\omega_{ij}^2}{2\tau_{ij}})
\textrm{exp}(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2}\tau_{ij})\}$$

$$\times\prod_{i=1}^p\textrm{exp}(\frac{\lambda}{2}\omega_{ij})\cdot1_{\Omega\in M^+}$$

- The restriction to the space of positive definite matrices makes this tricky,
but we can work with it.

# Deriving Gibbs Updates

For each update, partition $\Omega$, $S$, and $\mathcal{T} = \{\tau_{ij}\}_{\in D\times D}$

$$\Omega = \begin{bmatrix}
\Omega_{11} & \boldsymbol{\omega}_{12} \\
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}' & \omega_{22} 
\end{bmatrix} \space S = \begin{bmatrix}
S_{11} & \boldsymbol{s}_{12} \\
\boldsymbol{s}_{12}' & s_{22} 
\end{bmatrix} \space \mathcal{T} = \begin{bmatrix}
\mathcal{T}_{11} & \boldsymbol{\tau}_{12} \\
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{12}' & 0 
\end{bmatrix} \space$$

where $\Omega_{11}, S_{11}, \mathcal{T}_{11}$ are block matrices and $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}, \boldsymbol{s}_{12}, \boldsymbol{\tau}_{12}$ are length $D-1$ vectors containing the joint precisions, sample covariances, and scaling factors
for the current update. 

# Deriving Gibbs Updates

Then the conditional joint distribution of $(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12},\omega_{22})$
is 
$$p(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12},\omega_{22}|\Omega_{11},\mathcal{T},\textbf{Y},\lambda) \propto (\omega_{22} - \boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}' \Omega_{11}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12})^{\frac{n}{2}}$$

$$\times \textrm{exp}[-\frac{1}{2}\{\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}'D_{\tau}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12} + 2s'_{12}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12} + (s_{22}+\lambda)\omega_{22}\}]$$

by dropping out constant terms and using determinant properties.

We then make the change of variables
$$(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12},\omega_{22}) \to (\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}, \gamma = \omega_{22} - \boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}' \Omega_{11}^{-1}\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12})$$
resulting in new joint conditional . . .

# Deriving Gibbs Updates

$$(\gamma, \boldsymbol{\beta})|(\Omega_{11}, \mathcal{T}, \textbf{Y}, \lambda) \sim
\textrm{Gamma}(\frac{n}{2}+1, \frac{s_{22}+\lambda}{2}) \times 
\textrm{N}(-\textbf{C}s_{21}, \textbf{C})$$

where $\textbf{C} = \{(s_{22}+\lambda)\Omega_{11}^{-1} + D_{\tau}^{-1}\}^{-1}$

To sample from the full conditional of $\tau$, use:

Fact: Conditional posteriors of $u_{ij} = \frac{1}{\tau_{ij}}$ are independently inverse
Gaussian with parameters $\mu'=\sqrt{\lambda^2 / \omega_{ij}^2}, \lambda'=\lambda^2$.

Finally, we sample from the full conditional of our tuning parameter $\lambda$ given a prior $\lambda \sim \textrm{Gamma}(r, s)$:

$$\lambda \sim \textrm{Gamma}(r + D(D+1)/2, s + ||\Omega||_1/2)$$

# Final Algorithm

This leads to the algorithm for the Block Gibbs Sampler. Given current $\Omega$ and $\tau$:

1. For $i=1,...,D$
  (a) Partition $\Omega,S$ and $\mathcal{T}$ as shown
  (b) Sample $\gamma \sim (\Omega_{11}, \mathcal{T}, \textbf{Y}, \lambda) \sim \textrm{Gamma}(\frac{n}{2}+1, \frac{s_{22}+\lambda}{2})$ and 
$\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \textrm{N}(-\textbf{C}s_{21}, \textbf{C})$
  (c) Update $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{12}=\beta, \omega_{22} = \gamma + \beta'\Omega_{11}^{-1}\beta$

2. Sample $\lambda$ from $\lambda \sim \textrm{Gamma}(r + D(D+1)/2, s + ||\Omega||_1/2)$

3. For $i<j$, sample $u_{ij} \sim \textrm{Inv-Gau}(\mu',\lambda')$ and update
$\tau_{ij} = \frac{1}{u_{ij}}$

# Implementation and Simulation Study

- We will compare the performance of the Bayesian Graphical Lasso to its frequentist counterpart with a focus on edge classification 

- For Graphical Lasso, we simply say an edge exists between node $i$ and node $j$ if $\omega_{ij} \neq 0$. Any edges which shrunk to 0 are said to not exist.

- For the Bayesian Graphical Lasso, the probability of our posterior estimates being exactly 0 is 0, so we say an edge exists if a 95% credible interval about the posterior mean of $\omega_{ij}$ does not contain 0. 

- Finally, for each simulation, we train the Graphical Lasso with 10-fold cross validation and use its estimates of $\Omega$ and $\lambda$ as our initial Gibbs values (cheating).

# Data

- We have four simulation scenarios with $D \in {25,50,100,150}$ and max timepoints $T=200$. 

- To simulate functional MRI data, we generate scale-free networks with $D$ nodes then randomly rewire each node them with a small probability to mimic real brain networks.

- That graph is converted into an adjacency matrix and then each non-zero edge is randomly scaled to create a sparse precision matrix $\Omega_{true} \in D \times D$

- We then sample $T$ times from $MVN(0,\Omega_{true})$ to generate our simulated functional data.


# Simulation Results 

```{r, fig.show='hold', out.width="48%", out.height="48%"}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figures","graph_plot_D_25.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figures","graph_plot_D_50.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figures","graph_plot_D_100.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figures","graph_plot_D_150.png"))
```

# Simulation Results 

Each simulation had 1000 samples with 200 burn-in samples. The sample appeared to 
mix well quickly.

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "diag_trace.png")
)

```


# Simulation Results: Sampling

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "off_diag_trace.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Sampling

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "zero_off_diag_trace.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Edge Classification

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "sens.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Edge Classification

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "spec.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Stein Loss

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "stein.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Coverage

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "omega_cov.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Lambda

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "lambda.png")
)
```

# Simulation Results: Computation Time

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
knitr::include_graphics(
  here::here("figures", "comp.png")
)
```

# Challenges

- Beastly computation time for D > 150. Brain data even at coarse parcellations
has higher dimension.

- Extremely small values for $\tau$ matrices challenges invertibility

# Future Directions

- Speed up computation dramatically, probably via Rcpp, so that this can be
applied to real imaging data with higher dimension.

- Implement Bayesian Classification rule

- Bayesian Adaptive Graphical Lasso: allows for different $\lambda$'s at different edges. 

# References
